name: From Puebla de Guzmán to Santa Bárbara de Casa – circular through the Dehesa del Andévalo
name_original: Desde Puebla de Guzmán a Santa Bárbara de Casa – circular por la Dehesa del Andévalo
description: Cycle a difficult 46.0-mile loop through the Dehesa del Andévalo, gaining 2202 feet with views of cork oak forests and villages.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Puebla De Guzmán are set within the Campo del Andévalo, a region characterized by expansive, rolling landscapes and dehesas of holm oaks. The area features diverse natural elements, including the Rivera de la Cobica and the Embalse del Andévalo. Hills like Cerro del Águila, under 200 meters, offer panoramic views of the surrounding terrain. Best touring cycling routes around Puebla De Guzmán The most popular touring cycling route is From Puebla de Guzmán to Santa Bárbara de Casa – circular through the Dehesa del Andévalo, a 46.0 miles (74.0 km) trail that takes 5 hours 9 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers views of cork oak forests and villages. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Peña Maya loop from Ermita de Piedras Albas, a difficult 23.2 miles (37.3 km) path. This route winds through the varied landscapes of the Andévalo region, featuring significant elevation gain. Local touring cyclists also love the Vía Verde del Bajo Guadiana loop from Minas La Isabel, a 18.6 miles (29.9 km) trail leading through areas with mining heritage and diverse natural surroundings, often completed in about 1 hour 59 minutes. Touring cycling around Puebla De Guzmán is defined by rolling landscapes, dehesas of holm oaks, and river valleys. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Puebla De Guzmán are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.8 stars from several reviews. More than 100 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Puebla De Guzmán's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Puebla De Guzmán?
A: Puebla De Guzmán offers a good selection of touring cycling routes, with a total of 26 different tours available. These routes cater to various skill levels, including 4 easy, 11 moderate, and 11 difficult options.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Puebla De Guzmán?
A: The touring cycling routes in Puebla De Guzmán are characterized by expansive, rolling landscapes within the Campo del Andévalo. You'll encounter beautiful &lt;em&gt;dehesas&lt;/em&gt; of holm oaks, river valleys like the Rivera de la Cobica, and views of the Embalse del Andévalo. Hills such as Cerro del Águila offer panoramic vistas of the surrounding terrain.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Puebla De Guzmán?
A: Yes, there are several routes suitable for beginners and families. The region offers 4 easy touring cycling routes. One notable option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14620796" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rest area by the lake – Dirt road through the mines loop from Parque Estacionamento da Praia fluvial da Tapada Grande&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 15.7 km path, offering a pleasant experience without extreme difficulty.

Q: Are there challenging touring cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Puebla De Guzmán has 11 difficult routes. A popular choice is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e984968806/from-puebla-de-guzman-to-santa-barbara-de-casa-circular-through-the-dehesa-del-andevalo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Puebla de Guzmán to Santa Bárbara de Casa – circular through the Dehesa del Andévalo&lt;/a&gt;, a 74.0 km trail with significant elevation gain, offering views of cork oak forests and villages.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes offer stunning natural features. You can explore the copper lakes of the mine (&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7785262" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Copper lakes of the mine&lt;/a&gt;), the Ribeira do Vascão river (&lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/7782990" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ribeira do Vascão&lt;/a&gt;), and the expansive views from Cerro del Águila. The Molino de la Horca Natural Park also provides panoramic views of the Andévalo region with diverse trees like cork oaks and wild olive trees.

Q: Are there any historical sites or points of interest to explore while cycling?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history, particularly its mining heritage. You can visit the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2814217" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mina de São Domingos&lt;/a&gt;, the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5340752" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Achada do Gamo Copper Mine Ruins&lt;/a&gt;, or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5333422" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of La Peña of Puebla de Guzmán&lt;/a&gt;, located on Cerro del Águila.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in the area?
A: Yes, several circular routes are available. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24522725" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña Maya loop from Ermita de Piedras Albas&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 37.3 km path that winds through varied landscapes. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10318281" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vía Verde del Bajo Guadiana loop from Minas La Isabel&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 29.9 km route through areas with mining heritage.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Puebla De Guzmán?
A: The touring cycling routes in Puebla De Guzmán are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.8 stars from several reviews. Over 100 touring cyclists have explored the varied terrain, often praising the scenic landscapes and the diverse options for different skill levels.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Puebla De Guzmán?
A: The best time for touring cycling in Puebla De Guzmán is generally from October to May. During these months, the &lt;em&gt;dehesas&lt;/em&gt; of holm oaks present a stunning array of flowers and herbs, and the weather is typically more pleasant than the summer heat, making for a more enjoyable ride.

Q: Can I find places to rest or eat along the routes?
A: Yes, some routes pass by recreational areas. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6319282" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rest area by the lake&lt;/a&gt; (Tapada Grande) offers picnic tables, a café, and a river beach, providing a pleasant spot to relax and refuel during your ride.

Q: Are there options for parking near the touring cycling routes in Puebla De Guzmán?
A: While specific parking details vary by route, many routes start from or pass through towns and villages where parking is generally available. For routes near the Tapada Grande lake, there is a dedicated Parque Estacionamento da Praia fluvial da Tapada Grande, which serves as a convenient starting point.

Q: Are touring cycling routes in Puebla De Guzmán suitable for dogs?
A: Many outdoor routes in the region are generally dog-friendly, especially those on wider tracks and natural paths. However, it's always advisable to keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with livestock or protected wildlife, and to check specific route regulations if available.
tips:
The Church of the Holy Cross in Puebla de Guzmán (province of Huelva, Andalusia) is a temple of great historical, artistic, and spiritual value.

Original construction: Late 16th and early 17th centuries.
It was praised in 1645 by Francisco de Rada.
Between 1748 and 1757, significant renovations were carried out, including a new main chapel whose roof was converted into a watchtower.
It suffered severe damage during a fire in July 1936, at the beginning of the Spanish Civil War. It was rebuilt by the architect José María Pérez Carasa.
Views of the Malagón river from the bridge. The Malagón stream crosses the municipality of Paymogo from east to west, until it flows into the Ándevalo reservoir.

According to legend, the name of the municipality is due to a shaman who lived in the Sierra Morena of Huelva - his fame led these lands to be called "Country of the Magician", which ended up being shortened to the name Paymogo.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This circular touring bicycle route is approximately 74 km long and is categorized as difficult. It typically takes around 5 hours and 10 minutes to complete, though this can vary based on your fitness level and stops.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route traverses the distinctive Dehesa del Andévalo, featuring rolling hills, dense cork oak forests, and agricultural lands. While some sections may be on well-maintained asphalt, be prepared for potentially crumbling or poorly maintained surfaces, and some unpaved sections that can be challenging for a touring bicycle.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery will I encounter along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience diverse and picturesque scenery, including open agricultural fields, dense cork oak forests, and olive groves. Keep an eye out for Iberian pigs grazing in the dehesa meadows. The route offers views of the Rivera de Huelva and the mining basin from elevated points, showcasing the authentic Andalusian landscape.

faqs.question: Are there any specific points of interest or landmarks along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, notable points of interest include the Church of the Holy Cross in Puebla de Guzmán, the Malagón River, and the settlement of Paymogo. The emblematic Hermitage of Our Lady of La Peña on Cerro del Águila, near Puebla de Guzmán, is also a significant cultural landmark in the vicinity.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Spring is an excellent time to cycle this route, as it brings vibrant wildflowers and lush green landscapes, enhancing the visual appeal. The Huelva province generally offers pleasant cycling conditions outside of the hottest summer months.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is categorized as difficult for touring cycling. It requires very good fitness, involves significant elevation gains (over 670 meters), and can include challenging terrain. It is not recommended for beginners.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the start of the route?
faqs.answer: As the route starts and ends in Puebla de Guzmán, you can typically find parking within the town. Specific parking facilities for cyclists are not detailed, but street parking or public lots in the town center would be the most likely options.

faqs.question: Is public transport available to reach the trailhead?
faqs.answer: Information on specific public transport options directly to Puebla de Guzmán for cyclists is limited. It's advisable to check local bus schedules or regional transport services connecting to Puebla de Guzmán from larger towns in Huelva province.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route through the Dehesa del Andévalo. It is generally accessible to the public.

faqs.question: Is this route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the Dehesa del Andévalo is a natural area, specific regulations for dogs on this particular cycling route are not detailed. Given the length and difficulty, and potential for varied terrain, it might not be ideal for all dogs. If you bring a dog, ensure it is well-behaved, on a leash, and that you carry enough water for both of you.

faqs.question: Does the route intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Camino de los Peros, Camino de los Pocitos, Travesía Era Trasera, Camino de la Pared, and Camino Santa Rosalía.
